case_name: A.V. Janaki Amma And Ors. vs Union Of India (Uoi) And Ors.
compensation_amount: Rs. 3,00,000/- (apportioned: Petitioner 1: Rs. 50,000/-; Petitioner 2: Rs. 1,00,000/-; Petitioner 3: Rs. 1,00,000/-; Petitioner 4: Rs. 50,000/-)

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Smt. Savithri (deceased) / legal heirs (Petitioner Nos. 1 to 4)
compensation_breakdown.notes: Public law compensation under Article 226 for violation of Article 21; not punitive and not towards pecuniary/non-pecuniary damages; liberty reserved to file civil suit and adjustment directed; insurer not involved.

facts: Petitioners are the mother (petitioner 1), husband Major P. Gopalakrishna (petitioner 4), and daughters (petitioners 2 and 3) of late Smt. Savithri. Savithri was admitted to Military Hospital, Secunderabad and underwent cystectomy surgery for ovarian cyst on 26.11.1996 by respondent 2 (Obstetrician & Gynaecologist) assisted by Major Shyama Rao. Post-operatively she had persistent wound discharge and fever; despite a CT/ultrasound indicating a mass lesion and requests for referral for second opinion, the referral was rejected and follow-up was allegedly inadequate. On 01.01.1997 she was taken to Apollo Hospital, where exploratory laparotomy revealed a mop towel left in the abdomen from the earlier surgery; Savithri ultimately died on 16.02.1997 due to septicemia.
arguments_claimant: Petitioners contended that respondent doctors failed in their duty of care by leaving a mop/towel inside Savithri during surgery and by discharging her prematurely without proper follow-up despite adverse symptoms and scan findings. They argued the refusal to refer her to a specialist/other hospital worsened her condition, and the retained mop caused septicemia leading to death. They sought a declaration of illegality/arbitrariness and compensation in writ jurisdiction, asserting deprivation of Savithri’s right to life under Article 21 and claiming a constitutional tort remediable under Article 226.
arguments_respondent: Respondents (through a counter-affidavit filed on behalf of respondents 1 to 6) denied negligence and denied that a mop was left during surgery at Military Hospital, calling for strict proof. They attempted to shift blame to Apollo Hospital, even suggesting Apollo was responsible to pay compensation since Savithri died there. They justified non-referral to a private hospital by citing conditions/Army provisions and asserted requisite facilities and specialist officers were available at the Military Hospital.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that, on the Court of Inquiry record, “the principle of res ipso loquitor must be applied to the facts of this case” and that “Respondent Nos. 2 and 3 are grossly negligent in dealing with their patient Smt. Savithri,” with respondents 1 and 4 to 6 also not escaping “on the principle of vicarious liability.” It further held that “for violation of fundamental right guaranteed under Article 21 of the Constitution of India, public authorities, officials and State are liable to pay compensation” and that it is competent for High Courts under Article 226 “to award compensation in public law,” which “is only by way of applying balm to the injury” and is “in addition to the remedy in tort in private law.” Applying these principles, the Court concluded that Savithri “has been deprived of her fundamental right to life in most tragic manner” due to “gross negligence,” and therefore a mandamus should issue to the Union of India to pay compensation. The Court clarified: “this amount is not by way of punitive damages or is it towards pecuniary and non-pecuniary damages. This is for violation of fundamental right of Savithri, whose life was shortened by culpable negligence of Respondent Nos. 2 and 3,” and permitted adjustment against any civil court award and recovery by the Government from the negligent officers.
final_order: Writ petition allowed with directions: (i) first respondent to pay Rs. 3,00,000/- apportioned as Rs. 50,000/- each to petitioners 1 and 4 and Rs. 1,00,000/- each to petitioners 2 and 3; (ii) liberty to file civil suit, with adjustment of this amount against any civil court compensation and no recovery of this amount if civil suit fails on technical reasons; (iii) first respondent may recover Rs. 3,00,000/- from respondents 2 and 3 proportionately; and (iv) costs of Rs. 5,000/- to petitioners.
summary: A High Court writ petition was filed by the family of Smt. Savithri alleging that doctors at a Military Hospital in Secunderabad caused her death by negligence during surgery and follow-up care. The Court relied on the Court of Inquiry findings and held the doctors were grossly negligent and that the Union of India was vicariously liable. It ruled that compensation can be granted in writ jurisdiction for violation of the right to life, even though a separate civil suit for full damages is also available. The Court ordered the Union of India to pay Rs. 3,00,000/- (split among the family) and allowed the Government to recover that amount from the negligent doctors; it also granted liberty to file a civil suit with adjustment of this amount.
embedding_summary: A.V. Janaki Amma & Ors. v. Union of India (UOI) & Ors., decided on 23 October 2003 (Equivalent citation: 2004(1) ALD 19), judgment by V.V.S. Rao, J., arising from a writ petition under Article 226 seeking compensation for medical negligence/constitutional tort. Deceased Smt. Savithri (wife of Major P. Gopalakrishna; treated at Military Hospital, Secunderabad) underwent cystectomy/ovarian cyst surgery on 26.11.1996 by respondent doctor (Col. M.N. Nath) and assistant; persistent post-operative fever/wound discharge and scan findings allegedly ignored; referral requests rejected; she was later treated at Apollo Hospital Hyderabad where a surgical mop/towel was found; she died on 16.02.1997 of septicemia. Court applied res ipsa loquitur and relied on Court of Inquiry findings holding respondents 2 and 3 grossly negligent and held vicarious liability of Union of India/public authorities. Court held public law compensation is available for Article 21 violation (strict liability; sovereign immunity inapplicable), citing Rudul Shah, Nilabati Behera, D.K. Basu, Bhim Singh, Sebastian M. Hongray, Chairman Railway Board v. Chandrima Das, etc. Final relief: mandamus to Union of India to pay Rs. 3,00,000/- (P1 Rs.50k; P2 Rs.1 lakh; P3 Rs.1 lakh; P4 Rs.50k), liberty to file civil suit with adjustment, and permission to recover from negligent doctors; costs Rs. 5,000/-. Reimbursement of Apollo Hospital expenses (Rs. 2,99,241/- sanctioned by Govt. of India letter dated 30.09.2002) noted; no further order but subject to civil decree.
legal_principles: res ipsa loquitur applied to retained surgical mop (medical negligence inferred from facts)
vicarious liability of Union of India/public authorities for negligence of military hospital doctors
public law compensation under Article 226 for violation of Article 21 (constitutional tort)
strict liability in constitutional remedy; sovereign immunity inapplicable to Article 32/226 compensation claims
public law compensation is palliative (“applying balm”) and distinct from private law tort damages; adjustment against civil suit damages permitted
State may recover compensation paid from negligent public servants/officers proportionately

Rudul Shah v. State of Bihar

bm25_text: A.V. Janaki Amma And Ors. vs Union Of India (Uoi) And Ors. 2003 Petitioners are the mother (petitioner 1), husband Major P. Gopalakrishna (petitioner 4), and daughters (petitioners 2 and 3) of late Smt. Savithri. Savithri was admitted to Military Hospital, Secunderabad and underwent cystectomy surgery for ovarian cyst on 26.11.1996 by respondent 2 (Obstetrician & Gynaecologist) assisted by Major Shyama Rao. On 01.01.1997 she was taken to Apollo Hospital, where exploratory laparotomy revealed a mop towel left in the abdomen from the earlier surgery; Savithri ultimately died on 16.02.1997 due to septicemia. Petitioners contended that respondent doctors failed in their duty of care by leaving a mop/towel inside Savithri during surgery and by discharging her prematurely without proper follow-up despite adverse symptoms and scan findings. Respondents (through a counter-affidavit filed on behalf of respondents 1 to 6) denied negligence and denied that a mop was left during surgery at Military Hospital, calling for strict proof. The Court held that, on the Court of Inquiry record, “the principle of res ipso loquitor must be applied to the facts of this case” and that “Respondent Nos. 2 and 3 are grossly negligent in dealing with their patient Smt. This is for violation of fundamental right of Savithri, whose life was shortened by culpable negligence of Respondent Nos. 2 and 3,” and permitted adjustment against any civil court award and recovery by the Government from the negligent officers. Writ petition allowed with directions: (i) first respondent to pay Rs. 3,00,000/- apportioned as Rs. 50,000/- each to petitioners 1 and 4 and Rs. 1,00,000/- each to petitioners 2 and 3; (ii) liberty to file civil suit, with adjustment of this amount against any civil court compensation and no recovery of this amount if civil suit fails on technical reasons; (iii) first respondent may recover Rs. 3,00,000/- from respondents 2 and 3 proportionately; and (iv) costs of Rs. 5,000/- to petitioners. The Court ordered the Union of India to pay Rs. 3,00,000/- (split among the family) and allowed the Government to recover that amount from the negligent doctors; it also granted liberty to file a civil suit with adjustment of this amount.
